revision.text:
[[ja:誉津別命]] {{Infobox person|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|father={{ill|Suinin|qid=Q314850|1=Emperor Suinin}}|mother={{ill|Empress Sahohime|qid=Q11571754|1=Saho-hime}}|relatives={{ill|Keikō|qid=Q329723|1=Emperor Keikō}}; {{ill|Ojiwake no mikoto|qid=Q11620549|1=Ojiwake no mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Ikatarashihiko|qid=Q11372186|1=Prince Ikatarashihiko}}; {{ill|Prince Ikohayawake|qid=Q11492324|1=Prince Ikobayawake no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Inishikiirihiko|qid=Q11372194|1=Prince Inishikiirihiko}}; {{ill|Prince Iwatsukuwake|qid=Q11587884|1=Iwatsukiwake no Mikoto}}; {{ill|Prince Tuburame|qid=Q11394830|1=Prince Tuburame}}; {{ill|Yamatohime-no-mikoto|qid=Q4388732|1=Yamatohime-no-mikoto}}}} {{AFC submission|d|bio|u=Camillz|ns=118|decliner=Kline|declinets=20241010001848|reason2=npov|ts=20240821105040}} {{AFC submission|d|v|u=Camillz|ns=118|decliner=Asilvering|declinets=20240809050106|small=yes|ts=20240531170002}} ---- {{Short description|Japanese Prince, son of Emperor Suinin}} {{Draft topics|east-asia}} {{AfC topic|soc}} {{Infobox royalty | name = Homotsuwake no Mikoto
{{nobold|{{lang|ja|誉津別命}}}} | title = Prince | spouse = {{ill|Hinagahime|ja|ヒナガヒメ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinagahime|1=Hinagahime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q122375891}} | house = {{ill|Imperial House of Japan|en|Imperial House of Japan|qid=Q909452|lt=Imperial House of Japan|1=Imperial House of Japan}} | father = {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Emperor Suinin|1=Emperor Suinin}} | mother = [[Saho-hime]] }} '''Homotsuwake no Mikoto''' (誉津別命), is the Son of Emperor Suinin, and Saho-hime.{{Cite book |last=William George |first=Aston |author-link=William George Aston |url=https://en.m.wikisource.org/wiki/Nihongi:_Chronicles_of_Japan_from_the_Earliest_Times_to_A.D._697/Book_VI |title=Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697 |date=1896 |publisher=Society |isbn=978-0-524-05347-8}}{{Cite book |last=Yasumaro |url=https://ebin.pub/download/kojiki-an-account-of-ancient-matters-0231163894-9780231163897.html |title=Kojiki. An Account of Ancient Matters |date=2014 |translator-last=Heldt |translator-first=Gustav}} == Birth == In the [[Kojiki]], [[Saho-hime|his mother]] had not yet given birth. As she entered her brother's palace, labour began.{{Cite book |last=J.L |first=Kobe |url=https://religion-in-japan.univie.ac.at/k/images/2/2c/Kojiki_Chamberlain.pdf |title=Translation of the Kojiki. |date=1932 |publisher=Thompson & Co. |location=Chamberlain}} {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=The emperor|1=Emperor Suinin}} was attempting to burn her brother's due to an assassination attempt, but ordered his guards turn around and let Saho-hime give birth. Eventually the empress gave birth and told Emperor Suinin, that if he considered the child his own he could take the child himself. Yet as the emperor goes to retrieve his wife and new born son, his wife runs away, the boy left behind. The boy is retrieved and taken away. == Learning to speak == ===The Nihon Shoki version=== In both the [[Nihon Shoki]] and Kojiki, there is one main myth that involves him. By age 30 the prince did not speak and one day saw a swan, to which he finally spoke and said "what's this thing?" In the Nihon Shoki the emperor is so happy and asks someone to retrieve the swan. A man named Amano Yukaha Tana volunteers. Amano Yukaha Tana retrieves the swan, and Homotsuwake no Mikoto finally speaks. The emperor grants Amano Yukaha Tana the title of [[Kabane|Tottori no Miyakko]]. {{Referencesの明記|date=2015-12-27}} '''Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto''' (誉津別命, Homutsuwake-no-mikoto, [[生没年不詳]]) is a royal figure (or member of the ruling family) of the Kofun period who appears in works such as the {{ill|Kiki and Kokki|ja|記紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiki and Kokki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kiki (Shinto)|qid=Q842308}}. In the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}}, he is called Homutsuwake-no-mikoto, while in the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} he is referred to as either Honmuchiwake-no-mikoto or Shinmuchiwake-no-mikoto, and in an unclassified passage of the {{ill|Owari-kuni Fudoki|ja|尾張国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari-kuni Fudoki|qq=}} he is named Hinotsuwake-no-Ōji. He is the first son of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Suinin Tennō|1=Emperor Suinin}}, and his mother is the empress, {{ill|Saho-hime-no-mikoto|ja|狭穂毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho-hime-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (a daughter of {{ill|Hikozawa Ō|ja|日子坐王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hikozawa Ō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 彦坐王|1=Hikoimasu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11487910}}). In an unclassified passage of the {{ill|Kamiyomi-ki|ja|上宮記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kamiyomi-ki|1=Jōgūki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10867356}}, the name of a figure called Homutsuwake-no-Ō, normally identified with {{ill|Emperor Ōjin|en|Emperor Ōjin|qid=Q317997|lt=Ojin Tennō|1=Emperor Ōjin}} (also known as Homutsuwake-no-mikoto), appears as a forefather of {{ill|Emperor Keitai|en|Emperor Keitai|qid=Q349277|lt=Keitai Tennō|1=Emperor Keitai}}. == Tradition == === Birth in the Fire === [[File:NAJDA-158-0155 全像本朝古今列女伝1 (狹穗姬).jpg|thumb|Caption: Homutsuwake-no-Mikoto (lower left)]] According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, his name derives from the fact that he was born amidst flames in Inaji, so that his mother named him Honmuchiwake-no-mikoto. His mother, {{ill|Saho-hime-no-mikoto|ja|狭穂毘売命|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho-hime-no-mikoto|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, committed suicide during the rebellion initiated by her brother, {{ill|Saho-hiko-ō|ja|狭穂彦|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Saho-hiko-ō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 狭穂彦王|1=Sahohiko no Mikoto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11571755}}, during the 【Shaho-hiko no Hanran】 (狭穂毘古の反乱). Although the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} states that he was born before the rebellion, both texts agree that he was rescued from the fire. (It is said that in the 5th year of {{ill|Emperor Suinin|en|Emperor Suinin|qid=Q314850|lt=Suinin Tennō|1=Emperor Suinin}}, a command for his suppression was issued in October, but a siege at Inaji continued until the following month, during which a fire-attack was carried out. According to the Kojiki, Homutsuwake-no-mikoto’s birth is thus placed in November of Suinin Tennō’s 5th year.) === The Curse of Izumo Ōkami === Although Homutsuwake-no-Ōji (誉津別皇子) was deeply favored by his father, as he grew older his beard reached his chest but he still did not speak, and especially in the {{ill|Nihon Shoki|en|Nihon Shoki|qid=Q813935|lt=Nihon Shoki|1=Nihon Shoki}} he is described as crying like an infant. According to the Nihon Shoki, one day the prince saw a {{ill|hakuchō|ja|白鳥|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/hakuchō|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to ハクチョウ|1=swan|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q34384}} (swan) crossing and, for the first time, uttered the words “What is this thing?” The emperor rejoiced and ordered that the swan be captured. The ancestor of the Tottori-style builders, {{ill|Tenyukawaban-kyō|ja|天湯河板挙|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenyukawaban-kyō|1=Ame-no-Yukawata|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q52319907}}, captured the swan in {{ill|Izumo Province|ja|出雲|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Province|1=Izumo|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11395836}} (or, according to one account, in {{ill|Tajima Province|ja|但馬|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tajima Province|1=tajima|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11381620}}) and presented it to the court, and by playing with the swan Homutsuwake-no-mikoto eventually began to speak. At that time, divisions called the Tottori-bu, Torikai-bu, and Homutsu-bu were established. The {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}} offers a more detailed legend regarding the prince. The emperor constructed a two-forked boat from a cedar that split in two (a “futamata-fune”) brought from the land of {{ill|Owari Province|en|Owari Province|qid=Q514506|lt=Owari Province|1=Owari Province}}, floated it on the ponds of Ichishi and Karuki, and played with the prince aboard. On one occasion, the prince tried to say something when he saw a swan flying toward heaven, so the emperor ordered the swan to be captured. The swan flew over {{ill|Kii Province|en|Kii Province|qid=Q864053|lt=Kii Province|1=Kii Province}}, {{ill|Harima Province|en|Harima Province|qid=Q1047523|lt=Harima Province|1=Harima Province}}, {{ill|Inaba Province|ja|Inaba Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Inaba Province|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}, Tajima, {{ill|Ōmi Province|en|Ōmi Province|qid=Q843882|lt=Ōmi Province|1=Ōmi Province}}, {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, Owari, {{ill|Shinano Province|en|Shinano Province|qid=Q289250|lt=Shinano Province|1=Shinano Province}}, and {{ill|Koshi Province (Japan)|en|Koshi Province (Japan)|qid=Q1351611|lt=Echigo Province|1=Koshi Province (Japan)}} before finally being captured. However, even after obtaining the swan, the prince still did not speak. One night, someone appeared in the emperor’s dream and said, “If you rebuild my palace as your own palace, the prince will be able to speak.” The emperor then consulted a diviner (using {{ill|Futomani|en|Futomani|qid=Q5510530|lt=Taishō|1=Futomani}}) to determine who had appeared in his dream, and it was revealed that the silence (his inability to speak) was the result of a curse by {{ill|Izumo Ōkami|ja|出雲大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. The emperor sent the prince along with {{ill|Akebono Ō|ja|曙立王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Akebono Ō|1=Aketatsu no Ōkimi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11515056}} and {{ill|Utakami Ō|ja|菟上王|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Utakami Ō|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} to Izumo (present-day eastern {{ill|Shimane Prefecture|en|Shimane Prefecture|qid=Q132751|lt=Shimane Prefecture|1=Shimane Prefecture}}) to worship the deity. On the return from Izumo, when crossing the {{ill|Hiikawa|ja|肥川|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hiikawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, the emperor had a temporary palace constructed to lodge his entourage; there, an ancestor of the Izumo-kuni-zo (the progenitor of Izumo Province), {{ill|Kihisatsumi|ja|岐比佐都美|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kihisatsumi|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, decorated a tree with fresh green leaves and set it up downstream to present a meal. At that moment, the prince asked, “Is that which appears like a mountain of green leaves downstream truly a mountain? Or might it be the shrine of the deity, {{ill|Ashihara Sukihiro-Ōkami|ja|葦原色許男大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara Sukihiro-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who is served by the Izumo stone chamber in the former Sōmiya?” The accompanying kings rejoiced when the prince finally spoke, and when he was transferred to the binrō (【檳榔】) of Nagahomomiya, swift horses were set to run and report to the emperor. The emperor rejoiced, returned Utakami Ō to Izumo, and had the shrine of the Great Deity ({{ill|Izumo-taisha|en|Izumo-taisha|qid=Q696362|lt=Izumo Taisha|1=Izumo-taisha}}) built. It is also said that divisions called the Tottori-bu, Torikami-bu, Hinokochi-bu, Ōyu-zat, and Wakayuu-zat were established. An alternative tradition found in an unclassified passage of the {{ill|Owari Fudoki|ja|尾張国風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Owari Fudoki|qq=}} (attributed to the text of Izumo Fudoki) interprets the curse as coming from the deity {{ill|Amanoyamakatohime|ja|阿麻乃彌加都比女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanoyamakatohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}. According to this account, Homutsuwake-no-mikoto could not speak even at the age of 7, but in a dream the empress saw the deity of Taguni, {{ill|Amanoyamakatohime|ja|阿麻乃彌加都比女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanoyamakatohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, who said, “You have not yet been blessed (はふり); if someone is given to enshrine me on your behalf, you will be able to speak, and your lifespan will be extended.” Thereupon, the emperor had his ancestor, {{ill|Takeoka no Kun|ja|建岡君|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takeoka no Kun|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, divining where this deity resided. Takeoka no Kun went to Hanagakashiyama in {{ill|Mino Province|en|Mino Province|qid=Q864156|lt=Mino Province|1=Mino Province}}, broke a sacred 【ill|sakaki|ja|榊】 tree to make a hair ornament, and performed a ritual ({{ill|Ukei|ja|ウケイ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukei|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}) saying, “Surely the deity must reside where this ornament falls.” The ornament then flew through the air and landed in Owari Province in {{ill|Niwa District|en|Niwa District|qid=Q1347719|lt=Niwa District|1=Niwa District}}, so that Takeoka no Kun established a shrine there; later, the place came to be known as the village of Azura ({{ill|Azura Shrine|ja|阿豆良神社|lt=Azura|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Azura Shrine|1=Azura Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11657682}}) by a corruption of the name. Taguni is identified with the Takugawa River basin of Izumo Province, and {{ill|Amanoyamakatohime|ja|阿麻乃彌加都比女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Amanoyamakatohime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} is considered to be identical with the deity described in the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (in the section on {{ill|Qiūlu Gu|ja|秋鹿郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Qiūlu Gu|1=Aika district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11595889}}) of Ino, who is also identified with {{ill|Tenmō Kajihime|ja|天御梶日女|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tenmō Kajihime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} (or with the Tenmō Kajihime mentioned in the section on {{ill|Tatenui Hime|ja|楯縫郡神名樋山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Tatenui Hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid|qid=Q135579031|1=Tatenui Hime}}). Tenmō Kajihime is said to be the daughter of {{ill|Ashihara Sukihiro-Ōkami|ja|葦原色許男大神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ashihara Sukihiro-Ōkami|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, and her husband, {{ill|Achi Suzu Takahiko-no-Kami|ja|阿遅鉏高日子根神|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Achi Suzu Takahiko-no-Kami|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to アヂスキタカヒコネ|1=Ajisukitakahikone|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q506610}}, is, in the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, given a legend similar to that of Homutsuwake-no-mikoto—that even as an adult he did not cease to cry. === Encounter with Hino-hime === According to the {{ill|Kojiki|en|Kojiki|qid=Q813031|lt=Kojiki|1=Kojiki}}, later in Izumo the prince married {{ill|Hino-hime|ja|肥長比売|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hino-hime|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, but when he caught a glimpse of her, he saw that she had a serpent-like form and, filled with fear, fled. Then Hino-hime, illuminating the sea, pursued him; frightened still, the prince had his boat hauled up onto a mountain and escaped back to Yamato. == Various Theories == In mythological studies, these legends are compared with the myth in the {{ill|Kiki and Kokki|ja|記紀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiki and Kokki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|1=Kiki (Shinto)|qid=Q842308}} concerning {{ill|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|en|Susanoo-no-Mikoto|qid=Q272993|lt=Susanoo-no-Mikoto|1=Susanoo-no-Mikoto}}, who is said to have cried incessantly even after reaching adulthood, and with the myth in the {{ill|Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo-no-Kuni Fudoki|lt=Izumo Fudoki|ja|Izumo Province 風土記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Izumo Fudoki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} concerning {{ill|Ajisukitakahikone|en|Ajisukitakahikone|qid=Q506610|lt=Ajisukitakahikone|1=Ajisukitakahikone}}, who was unable to speak. {{Citation needed範囲|date=2015th year 8月2日 (日) 01:26 (UTC)|火中出産は{{ill|Konohananosakuyahime|ja|木花咲耶姫|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Konohananosakuyahime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to コノハナノサクヤビメ|1=Konohanasakuya-hime|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q1781862}}’s {{ill|Ukei|ja|誓約|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ukei|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 宣誓|1=Oath|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q381045}}につながるとの指摘がある。}} == Footnotes == {{reflist}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of Pre-Emperors before the Asuka Period|ja|飛鳥時代以前の人物一覧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Pre-Emperors before the Asuka Period|1=list of ancient Japanese figures|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11665973}} * {{ill|Kibi no Shihokoku-zukuri|ja|吉備品治国造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kibi no Shihokoku-zukuri|qq=}} * {{ill|Hinokogun|ja|品治郡|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Hinokogun|1=Honji district|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10921834}} == Refrences == {{Drafts moved from mainspace |date=May 2024}} [[Category:Year_of_birth_unknown]] [[Category:Year_of_death_unknown]]
